blocks.text: When Israel went out from Egypt, the house of Jacob from a people of strange language,

Ps 114: The wonder of the Exodus. A psalm with hymnlike features, celebrating the Exodus from Egypt and entry into the land. An apt Hallel psalm ((Pss 113–118) with standard synonymous parallelism and internal repetition (vv. 3–4; 5–6).
Judah became God’s sanctuary, Israel his dominion.
The sea looked and fled; Jordan turned back.
3 : is parallel to and is personified as a defeated warrior (cf. Pss 77.16; 104.7). The taunts sea and mountains as they tremble and flee (Ps 96.9; Deut 2.25).
The mountains skipped like rams, the hills like lambs.

1–4 : Somewhat abruptly, the psalm describes Israel leaving Egypt, passing through the sea (see (Ex 14–15) to a safe land. Telescoping events, this poem has the people cross from Egypt over the Jordan directly into Canaan.
Why is it, O sea, that you flee? O Jordan, that you turn back?
O mountains, that you skip like rams? O hills, like lambs?
Tremble, O earth, at the presence of the Lord, at the presence of the God of Jacob,
who turns the rock into a pool of water, the flint into a spring of water.
8 : Possibly a reference to God providing water in the wilderness from a as in Ps 78.15,20; Ex 17.6; Num 20.11.
